title: Use metrics in search
content:
Home
Documentation
Security
Google Security Operations
Guides
Stay organized with collections

Use metrics in search
Use metrics in search
Supported in:
Google secops
SIEM
This document outlines the application of metrics functions within Search, a feature designed to facilitate the analysis of aggregated historical behavioral data using YARA-L 2.0. These functions let you derive high-level insights from large datasets by incorporating them directly into the
outcome
section of a search query.
Function parameters
Metrics functions can perform entity behavioral analytics within your searches. For example, you can construct a search query to determine the maximum number of daily bytes a specific IP address has sent within the past month. To represent the specific IP address, you can directly input the IP value in your search query instead of using placeholder variables.
Due to the large number of arguments used in these functions, they use named parameters, which can be specified in any order. The parameters are as follows:
Period
The length of time over which individual log events are combined into a single observation. The only values allowed are
1h
and
1d
.
Window
The length of time over which individual observations are aggregated into a single value, such as the average and the maximum. The allowed values for the window are based on the metric period. The valid mappings are as follows:
period:1h
:
window:today
period:1d
:
window:30d
Example: The following query returns the first and last time a user logged in successfully in a given day, over the last 30 days.
$u1.principal.asset.ip = $ip
    $ip = "10.138.0.4"
    $user = $u1.target.user.userid

  match:
    $ip, $user

  outcome:

    // First and last time a user logged in successfully

     $first_seen = max(metrics.auth_attempts_success(
        period:1d, window:30d,
        metric:first_seen,
        agg:min,
        target.user.userid:$user))

    $last_seen = max(metrics.auth_attempts_success(
        period:1d, window:30d,
        metric:last_seen,
        agg:max,
        target.user.userid:$user))
Metrics
Within each period, each observation has a number of metrics associated with it. One of these must be selected for aggregation over the entire window. The supported metric types are:
event_count_sum
—Number of unique log events within each period.
first_seen
—First seen timestamp of a matching log event within each period.
last_seen
—Last seen timestamp of a matching log event within each period.
value_sum
—Sum of the number of bytes in all the log events combined within the period. You can only use this value for metrics functions with bytes in the name.
num_unique_filter_values
—Metric that isn't pre-computed by Google SecOps but can be computed during search query execution. See
Count unique metrics
for more details and requirements.
Aggregations
Aggregations are applied over the entire window (for example, the highest daily value over the past 30 days). The allowed values are:
avg
—Average value per period. This is a statistical mean that doesn't include zero values.
max
—Greatest value per period.
min
—Smallest value per period.
num_metric_periods
—Number of periods within the time window that had a non-zero metric value.
stddev
—Standard deviation of the value per period. This is a statistical standard deviation which doesn't include zero values.
sum
—Sum of each value per period, over the entire window.
earliest
—Timestamp of the first (oldest) event with microsecond resolution.
latest
—Timestamp of the last (most recent) event with microsecond resolution.
For examples, see
Statistics and aggregations in UDM search using YARA-L 2.0
.
Filter
Filters allow filtering metrics before aggregation by a value in the pre-computed metric (see values in
Metric
). Filters can be any valid event expression (a single line in the event section) which doesn't contain any event fields or placeholders. The only variables that can be included in this condition are metric types.
Example: The following outcome section of a query returns the maximum number of daily bytes that a specific IP address has sent in the past month.
$ip = principal.asset.ip
match:
    $ip
outcome:
$max_bytes_per_day = max(metrics.network_bytes_outbound(
    period: 1d, window: 30d,
    metric: value_sum,
    agg:max,
    principal.asset.ip: $ip,
    filter:value_sum > 10 AND event_count_sum > 2))
UDM fields
Metrics are filtered by one, two, or three UDM fields, depending on the function. For information on functions, see
Functions
.
The following types of UDM fields are used for metrics functions:
Dimension (Required): Different combinations are listed on this page. You can't join a metric with a default value (
""
for string and
0
for int).
Namespaces (Optional): You can only use namespaces for entities that you specify in dimensions. For example, if you use
principal.asset.hostname
filter, you can use a
principal.namespace
filter as well. If you don't include a namespace filter, the data across all namespaces is aggregated together. You can use a default value as a namespace filter.
Window calculations
Google SecOps calculates metrics using either a daily or hourly metric
window.
Daily windows
All daily windows, such as
30d
, are determined the same way.
Google SecOps uses the latest available metrics data that has been
generated that doesn't overlap with the search query time range. Calculation of the
daily metrics can take up to 6 hours to complete and doesn't start until the end
of the day in UTC. Metric data for the day before is available at or
before 6:00 UTC each day.
For example, for a search query that is running over event data from 2023-10-31 4:00 UTC
to 2023-10-31 7:00 UTC, the daily metrics for 2023-10-30 is likely
generated, so the metric calculation uses the data from 2023-10-01 to
2023-10-30 (inclusive). Whereas for a search query that is running over event data from
2023-10-31 1:00 UTC to 2023-10-31 3:00 UTC, the daily metrics for 2023-10-30
is likely not generated, so the metric calculation uses the data
from 2023-09-30 to 2023-10-29 (inclusive).
Hourly
today
window
The hourly metric window is calculated differently from the window for
daily metrics. The hourly metric window of
today
is not a static size like the
30d
window for daily metrics. The hourly metric window
today
fills in as
much data as possible between the end of the daily window and the start of the
search query time window.
For example, for a search query that is running over event data from 2023-10-31 4:00:00
UTC to 2023-10-31 7:00:00 UTC, the daily metric calculation uses the data
from 2023-10-01 to 2023-10-30 (inclusive) and the hourly metric window uses
data from 2023-10-31 00:00:00 UTC to 2023-10-31 4:00:00 UTC.
Count unique metrics
The metric
num_unique_filter_values
is not pre-computed by Google SecOps.
It is computed when the search query is run. This is done by aggregating
over an existing dimension in a pre-computed metric. For example, the metric
"daily total count of distinct countries that a user attempted to authenticate in"
can be derived from the pre-computed
auth_attempts_total
metric on the
dimensions
target.user.userid
and
principal.ip_geo_artifact.location.country_or_region
by performing a count
unique aggregation over the latter dimension.
Example:
$userid = target.user.userid
match:
    $userid
outcome:
$outcome_variable = max(metrics.auth_attempts_total(
    period: 1d,
    window: 30d,
    // This metric type indicates any filter with a wildcard value should be
    // aggregated over each day to produce a new metric dynamically
    metric: num_unique_filter_values,
    agg: max,
    target.user.userid: $userid,
    // Filter whose value should be counted over each day to produce the
    // num_unique_filter_values metric.
    principal.ip_geo_artifact.location.country_or_region: *
))
Limitation: Computing unique metric count can only be aggregated over a single filter
dimension. This is indicated by using the wildcard token
*
as the filter value.
Functions
This section includes documentation on the specific metrics functions supported
by Google SecOps.
Alert Events
metrics.alert_event_name_count
precomputes historical values for UDM events
that have had alerts generated by Carbon Black, CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft
Graph API Alerts, or Microsoft Sentinel.

Authentication Attempts
metrics.auth_attempts_total
precomputes historical values for UDM events with
a
USER_LOGIN
event
type
.
metrics.auth_attempts_success
further requires that the event had at least one
SecurityResult.Action
of
ALLOW
.
metrics.auth_attempts_fail
requires instead that none of the
SecurityResult.Actions
were
ALLOW
.

DNS Bytes Outbound

precomputes historical values for UDM events where
network
.sent_bytes
is greater than 0,
and the target port is
53/udp
,
53/tcp
, or
3000/tcp
.
network
.sent_bytes
is
available as
value_sum
.

).

dns.response_code
greater than

.

metrics.file_executions_total
precomputes historical values for UDM events
with a
PROCESS_LAUNCH
event
type
.
metrics.file_executions_success
further requires that the event had at least
one
SecurityResult.Action
of
ALLOW
.
metrics.file_executions_fail
instead requires that none of the
SecurityResult.Actions
were
ALLOW
.

network

is less than 400.

.
http.response_code
is greater than or equal to 400.

Network Bytes
metrics.network_bytes_inbound
precomputes historical values for UDM events
that have a non-zero value for
network
.
received_bytes
,
and makes that field available as
value_sum
.
metrics.network_bytes_outbound
requires a non-zero value for
network
.
sent_bytes
, and
makes that field available as
value_sum
.
metrics.network_bytes_total
considers events that have a non-zero value for
either
network
.received_bytes
or
network
.sent_bytes
(or
both), and make the sum of those two fields available as
value_sum
.

Resource Creation
metrics.resource_creation_total
precomputes historical values for UDM events
with a
RESOURCE_CREATION
event
type
or a
USER_RESOURCE_CREATION
event
type
.
For a list of equivalent event types, see
Metadata Event Types
metrics.resource_creation_success
further requires that the event have at
least one
SecurityResult.Action
of
ALLOW
.

.

Limitations
When creating YARA-L search queries with metrics, the following limitations apply:
You can't join a metric with a default value (
""
for string and
0
for int).
Default values:
If there is no metric data that corresponds to an event, the returned value from the metrics function is
0
.
If there is an event which has no metric data, using
min
to aggregate over the function might return
0
.
Metrics functions can only be used in the outcome section. They must be aggregated in search queries with a match section.
